The **governor of New York** is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"). The governor is the head of the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") of [New York's state government](/wiki/New_York%27s_state_government "New York's state government") and the [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the state's [military](/wiki/New_York_State_Division_of_Military_and_Naval_Affairs "New York State Division of Military and Naval Affairs") forces. The [governor](/wiki/Governor_(United_States) "Governor (United States)") has a duty to enforce state laws and the power to either approve or [veto](/wiki/Veto "Veto") bills passed by the [New York Legislature](/wiki/New_York_Legislature "New York Legislature"), to convene the legislature and grant [pardons](/wiki/Pardon "Pardon"), except in cases of [impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment_in_the_United_States "Impeachment in the United States") and [treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason"). The governor of New York is the highest paid governor in the country.

The current governor is [Kathy Hochul](/wiki/Kathy_Hochul "Kathy Hochul"), a member of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") who took office on August 24, 2021, following the resignation of [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo").[[1]](#cite_note-Hochul_sworn_in-1) She was [elected](/wiki/2022_New_York_gubernatorial_election "2022 New York gubernatorial election") to a full term in 2022.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_of_New_York&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Governor_of_New_York&action=edit&section=). *(August 2024)* |

The position of governor in New York dates back to the British takeover of New Amsterdam, when the position replaced the former Dutch offices of director or director-general.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

The *[New York State Constitution](/wiki/New_York_State_Constitution "New York State Constitution")*, section XVII originates the role, reading:

> "And this convention doth further, in the name and by the authority of the good people of this State, ordain, determine, and declare that the supreme executive power and authority of this State shall be vested in a governor; and that statedly, once in every three years, and as often as the seat of government shall become vacant, a wise and descreet freeholder of this State shall be, by ballot, elected governor..."[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

## Election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_of_New_York&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Election")]

See also: [New York gubernatorial elections](/wiki/New_York_gubernatorial_elections "New York gubernatorial elections")

The governor is directly elected every four years, in even-numbered years when there is no presidential election.[[4]](#cite_note-NYSConstitution-4) The governor is required to be a United States citizen and a resident of New York for five years preceding their election. No person can be elected as governor under the age of thirty.[[4]](#cite_note-NYSConstitution-4)

## Powers and duties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_of_New_York&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Powers and duties")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/John_Jay_Certificate_of_Election_as_Gov._of_NY_1795.jpg/250px-John_Jay_Certificate_of_Election_as_Gov._of_NY_1795.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Jay_Certificate_of_Election_as_Gov._of_NY_1795.jpg)

The original Certificate of Election of John Jay as Governor of New York (June 6, 1795)

The governor has a duty to enforce state laws, and the power to either approve or [veto](/wiki/Veto_power_in_the_United_States#In_state_government "Veto power in the United States") [bills](/wiki/Bill_(law) "Bill (law)") passed by the [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature"), to convene the legislature, and to grant [pardons](/wiki/Pardon "Pardon"), except in cases of [treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason") and [impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment_in_the_United_States "Impeachment in the United States").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Unlike the other government departments that compose the executive branch of government, the governor is the head of the state [Executive Department](/wiki/New_York_State_Executive_Department "New York State Executive Department"). The officeholder is afforded the courtesy style of *Their [Excellency](/wiki/Excellency "Excellency")* while in office.[[6]](#cite_note-google-6)

Often considered a potential candidate for [U.S. president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), ten New York governors have been selected as presidential candidates by a major party, four of whom ([Martin Van Buren](/wiki/Martin_Van_Buren "Martin Van Buren"), [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland"), [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), and [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt")) were elected as [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"). Meanwhile, six New York governors have gone on to serve as [vice president](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Additionally, two New York governors, [John Jay](/wiki/John_Jay "John Jay") and [Charles Evans Hughes](/wiki/Charles_Evans_Hughes "Charles Evans Hughes"), have served as [chief justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States").

## Appointments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_of_New_York&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Appointments")]

The governor is responsible for appointing their Executive Chamber. These appointments do not require the confirmation of the [New York State Senate](/wiki/New_York_State_Senate "New York State Senate"). Most political advisors report to the secretary to the governor, while most policy advisors report to the director of state operations, who also answers to the secretary to the governor, making that position, in practice, the true chief of staff and most powerful position in the Cabinet.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The actual "chief of staff" is in charge of the Office of Scheduling and holds no authority over other cabinet officials.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The governor is also charged with naming the heads of the various departments, divisions, boards, and offices within the state government. These nominees require confirmation by the state Senate. While some appointees may share the title of *commissioner*, *director*, etc., only department level-heads are considered members of the actual state cabinet, although the heads of the various divisions, boards, and offices may attend cabinet-level meetings from time to time.

## Line of succession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Governor_of_New_York&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Line of succession")]

See also: [Gubernatorial lines of succession in the United States § New York](/wiki/Gubernatorial_lines_of_succession_in_the_United_States#New_York "Gubernatorial lines of succession in the United States")

The Constitution of New York has provided since 1777 for the election of a [lieutenant governor of New York](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_of_New_York "Lieutenant governor of New York"), who also acts as president of the State Senate, to the same term (keeping the same term lengths as the governor throughout all the constitutional revisions). Originally, in the event of the death, resignation or impeachment of the governor, or absence from the state, the lieutenant governor would take on the governor's duties and powers. Since the 1938 constitution, the lieutenant governor explicitly becomes governor upon such vacancy in the office.

Should the office of lieutenant governor become vacant, the [temporary president](/wiki/President_pro_tempore "President pro tempore") of the state senate[[10]](#cite_note-10) performs the duties of a lieutenant governor until the governor can take back the duties of the office, or the next election; likewise, should both offices become vacant, the temporary president acts as governor, with the office of lieutenant governor remaining vacant. Although no provision exists in the constitution for it, [precedent set in 2009](/wiki/Richard_Ravitch#Lieutenant_Governor "Richard Ravitch") allows the governor to appoint a lieutenant governor should a vacancy occur.[[11]](#cite_note-CoA-11) Should the temporary president be unable to fulfill the duties, the speaker of the assembly is next in the line of succession. The lieutenant governor is elected on the same [ticket](/wiki/Ticket_(election) "Ticket (election)") as the governor, but nominated separately.

Line of succession:

1. [Lieutenant Governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_New_York "Lieutenant Governor of New York")
2. [Temporary President of the Senate](/wiki/Majority_Leader_of_the_New_York_State_Senate "Majority Leader of the New York State Senate")
3. [Speaker of the Assembly](/wiki/List_of_Speakers_of_the_New_York_State_Assembly "List of Speakers of the New York State Assembly")
4. [Attorney General](/wiki/Attorney_General_of_New_York "Attorney General of New York")
5. [Comptroller](/wiki/New_York_State_Comptroller "New York State Comptroller")
6. Commissioner of Transportation[[12]](#cite_note-12)
7. [Commissioner of Health](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Health_of_the_City_of_New_York "Commissioner of Health of the City of New York")
8. Commissioner of Commerce[[13]](#cite_note-13)
9. Industrial Commissioner[[14]](#cite_note-14)
10. [Chairman of the Public Service Commission](/wiki/New_York_Public_Service_Commission "New York Public Service Commission")
11. [Secretary of State](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_of_New_York "Secretary of State of New York")[[15]](#cite_note-15)
